This feels a little bit hackish to me. I assume you added the eps to account for the differences in P2 and P3 round() behaviour (banker's rounding vs. to nearest even)?

I think a more elegant solution to the P2 rounding behaviour in P3 would be:
```
import decimal
decimal.Decimal(x / 2.).quantize(decimal.Decimal('1'), rounding=decimal.ROUND_HALF_UP)
```

What do you think?

I don't like the idea of the dependency either, however the main use-case of _acme-dns_ is pointing a `CNAME` record to the _acme-dns_ instance from the main domain. So for example, in case of `hsbp.org`, I've set it up like this: (`auth.vsza.hu` is my private _acme-dns_ instance)

```
$ host -a _acme-challenge.hsbp.org.
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 41523
;; flags: qr rd ra; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 2, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 0

;; QUESTION SECTION:
;_acme-challenge.hsbp.org.      IN      ANY

;; ANSWER SECTION:
_acme-challenge.hsbp.org. 3599  IN      CNAME   78d4dd08-c13d-4700-acdc-2bd2a7b3f2ef.auth.vsza.hu.
```

You're right, I got `78d4dd08-c13d-4700-acdc-2bd2a7b3f2ef` from the response to the `register` call, however, I did that only once, as the whole point that _acme-dns_ solves is that I don't want to update the `_acme-challenge` entry, I just want to set it up once to point to _acme-dns. And it seemed to me that the Python standard library doesn't have the bits to perform the necessary DNS queries, since `gethostbyname{,_ex}` relies on `A` and `AAAA` records only.

If you really dislike the dependency, I see several ways, some of them could even be combined:

 - Put it as an _extra_ dependency, since it's only used by the _acme-dns_ module (and let's face it, the zeitgeist is people using cloud magic, self-hosters like me are a minority, even if a vocal one).
 - Fall back to calling `host(1)`, `nslookup(1)` or `dig(1)` using `subprocess` (ugly but it can work).
 - Let the user specify the `subdomain` (like after 6ead878 but before 85aabfe), although this might cause some problems if there are multiple domains specified using `domain_alt_names` and not all might point to the same _acme-dns_ instance.

I have to disagree -- there is neither a row of constants nor a column of constants.  Only the very first coefficient, [0, 0] is a constant, a zero-power coefficient.  The remaining entries in the top row and left column are first-power low-frequency coefficients in each axis.  This is why I keep saying we should use [:8, :8] (or / hash_size, whatever you all want) instead of trying to offset by a row or column.  Please see my issue report for more details:  https://github.com/JohannesBuchner/imagehash/issues/13

Another thing to keep in mind: it's unlikely that any two implementations of a fuzzy image hash will ever return the same hash value as each other.  A lot of differences stack up:  do we convert to greyscale and then resize down, or resize down and then convert to greyscale; what antialiasing algorithm is used; what endian-ness is used when converting the array of coefficients to bits in an integer; and probably also issues of simple numerical precision and handling in the low-level libraries.

We need this check because `flag_upstream_failed` will change the state of tasks but still return a fail condition.

There is an issue that the dependency model only has True/False for dependencies being met but upstream_failed and upstream skipping are cases where the dependency isn't met per se but that we don't want to count it against the dag moving forward.

Basically, the task is blocked but the DAG is not, which is a level of granularity the current boolean return doesn't account for.

Longer term, it might make sense to have a richer possible return state for dependency checks but, in the short term resolving the deadlock issue was my priority.

Thanks for explaining! The scenario you described is definitely a common case.

However, from SDK point of view, we should be more careful.
Before this change, the function execute has three possible outcomes:
1. succeed: execute _success_callbacks if exist.
2. no response: add to the returned batch.
3. fail: execute _failure_callbacks

With this PR, we're adding one more case: transient failure, which has a total different behavior than any of the original one; it will be added to the batch, but it'll also trigger the failure_callback.

Transient failure is not the same as total fail, but there's no option for callers to distinguish what they want to do for total failure and transient failure.

So we have three options here:
1. categorize transient failure with one of the original outcome and align with the original outcome behavior, which will be, just adding to the retry batch and not call failure_callback.
2. categorize transient failure with one of the original outcome and change the original outcome behavior with the transient failure, which will be, call failure_callbacks even with no response result.
3. add a new outcome. In this case, it'll be adding another __transient_error_callbacks.

Option 3 gives any caller the most flexibility to do whatever they want.

python2 using `__future__`
```python
from __future__ import division

Then, let's think about providing this functionality out of the box. We can convert the `enable_task_linking` function so that it's similar to what we usually do for other integrations:
* Tornado [example](https://github.com/thehesiod/dd-trace-py/blob/b9a41ec6cd7f8636cdbddb2134eaab88604f085e/ddtrace/contrib/tornado/patch.py#L11-L56)
* Gevent [example](https://github.com/thehesiod/dd-trace-py/blob/b9a41ec6cd7f8636cdbddb2134eaab88604f085e/ddtrace/contrib/gevent/patch.py#L12-L32)

So having a generic `patch()` function that is not called automatically. However, when it is, it could force the propagation between different Tasks. Also I think it could be great having the `patch()` and `unpatch()` idempotent like https://github.com/thehesiod/dd-trace-py/blob/b9a41ec6cd7f8636cdbddb2134eaab88604f085e/ddtrace/contrib/tornado/patch.py#L16-L19. So we don't cause an assert error if called twice.

In that way we can also add even more tests:
* using the helper to propagate the `Context` manually
* without the helper the propagation must not take place
* after calling the `patch()` the propagation must take place automatically
* after calling the `patch()` and then `unpatch()` the propagation must not take place

I think that these changes are reasonable so we have:
* a helper to manually propagate the `Context`
* a `patch()` method that patches asyncio (so developers are aware of that)
* the `patch()` is not called by default (so no huge / breaking changes after this merge)

I think this has to be sharped up a bit. In my mind the exact modules that does the job, is not so important as compared to the underlying technology actually used. E.g. JWT is not so important to mention - it's that we use a shorted lived CSRF-token (which happen to be implemented with JWT) which is important.

I know it's pretty hairy and difficult so I've tried to write it up:

----------

A user can make authenticated requests against the Invenio REST APIs using two different methods:

1. **Session** 

A user who logged into an Invenio application in the browser obtains a session. The session is implemented via Secure HTTP-only cookies to ensure the cookie containing the session identifier is only submitted over HTTPS and that JavaScript applications running in the browser cannot access the cookie. When the session cookie is provided in an HTTP request against the API, the cookie is used to authenticate the user. 

Because the session-based authentication is primarily used from a browser, it is important to protect the API against Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks. Invenio protects against CSRF-attacks by embedding a shorted lived CSRF-token into the HTML DOM tree from the server-side. This CSRF-token is then read by a JavaScript application and added to the HTTP request header. Thus the HTTP request header will include both the session cookie as well as the CRSF-token. The CSRF-token is implemented via a JSON Web Token (JWT).

The session identifier stored inside the session cookie is furthermore protected in a way so that it must be used from the same machine and same browser. 

2. **Access token**: 

An access token (or API key) can also be used to make authenticated requests against the API. Access tokens are primarily used by machines accessing the Invenio REST API, contrary to session-based authentication which is primarily used in browsers by humans. The access tokens can also be used to delegate user rights to a third-party application without exposing user credentials. This delegation of rights can further be scoped to specific parts of the API to not give full access to third-party applications.

Access tokens can be obtained in different ways. A user may for instance manually create an access token via the user interface, or e.g. a third-party application can initiate an OAuth 2.0 authentication flow that eventually provides them with an access token. The different scenarios for how to obtain an access token are explained in detail further below.

Playing around with that, it confirms that polarity isn't necessarily just -1, 0, or 1.

As @tseaver mentioned, polarity and magnitude both provide insight into the sentiment of a text blob. Polarity gives the overall negativity / positivity, and magnitude gives a sense of how emotional in general the text blob was. Conceptually, magnitude is roughly `sum(abs(sentence.polarity) for sentence in text_blob)`. Side note - magnitude scales with the length of the text, whereas polarity doesn't.

I think that this should be converted to a `remote_rpm_source`. This is just the situation it was written for, it will download the file from a known namespace and cache it permanently.

Note that this downgrades to the default golang version from the go contrib module - there should someday be a first-class way to associate these in pants.ini.

```
diff --git a/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/BUILD b/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/BUILD
index 85020bd06..3d72d2607 100644
--- a/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/BUILD
+++ b/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/BUILD
@@ -1,12 +1,17 @@
-golang_rev = '1.9.2'
+golang_rev = '1.8.3'
+
+remote_rpm_source(
+  name='go.tar.gz',
+  version=golang_rev,
+)

 rpm_package(
   name='golang',
   spec='golang.spec',
-  remote_sources=[
-    'https://storage.googleapis.com/golang/go{}.linux-amd64.tar.gz'.format(golang_rev),
-  ],
   defines={
     'version': golang_rev,
   },

 )
diff --git a/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/golang.spec b/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/golang.spec
index fa7509215..9bf8edb3e 100644
--- a/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/golang.spec
+++ b/contrib/rpmbuild/examples/golang.spec
@@ -2,6 +2,8 @@
 # confused by prebuilt Go binaries.
 %global debug_package %{nil}

+%define golang_source_pkg go.tar.gz
+
 Summary: Go language
 Name: golang
 Version: %{version}
@@ -15,7 +17,7 @@ BuildRequires: tar
 # Disable RPM's standard automatic dependency detection since the Go binaries are static.
 AutoReqProv: no

-Source: go%{version}.linux-amd64.tar.gz
+Source: %{golang_source_pkg}

 BuildRoot: %{_tmppath}/%{name}-%{version}-%{release}-root-%(%{__id_u} -n)

 %install
 rm -rf "%{buildroot}"

FWIW Python provides a nice way of dealing with errno values:

```python
>>> import os
>>> os.strerror(3)
'No such process'
```

So you could write this as:

```python
import errno
# ...
try:
    os.kill(pid, self.kill_signal)
except OSError as oe:
    if oe.errno == errno.ESRCH:
        print("PID {}: {}".format(pid, os.strerror(oe.errno))
    else:
        raise
```

Admittedly ESRCH isn't the most human-friendly, but it's a little more explicit. The other error code aren't quite as bad: https://docs.python.org/2/library/errno.html

That's basically the condition under which it would fail because of the comparison anyway, and I don't want to enforce that it's specifically `tzutc` (what if you used `datetime.timezone.utc` or `pytz.UTC`). I think being slightly less strict about the specifics of the RFC is worth it in this case, since I want to tread lightly.

I'll note that there's a very strange edge case that would occur if `self._dtstart.tzinfo is self._until.tzinfo` and the UNTIL value is an ambiguous time, but I'm not terribly worried about that.

The specific failure case (that I have encountered on other projects) I am trying to prevent is:

A user, while hacking round on their application code, end up sticking in a bunch of inits in all different files.

Then six months later, when they've forget that they've done that, they modify one to include some different config options.

Then they spend a long time trying to understand why their config options aren't being applied.

For LSTM/RNN/GRU, I think the existence of optional outputs (or inputs) means that an operator can have multiple static configurations because whether the first output is computed or not is determined by a user-specified attribute. Therefore, the user of such an operator should be in charge of choosing the right outputs. If she turns output_sequence on, the first output name would be a meaningful string. If not, she can use an empty string. My point is that as long as an output configuration is (implicitly) determined by an user, using an empty string is better than setting up a flag+provide an uncomputed tensor name.

This is not a good API, I'm afraid. In a good API, the meaning of arguments should be understandable without having any context about the underlying implementation, only from domain-relevant information. Meaning that, in this case, someone who is familiar with MobileNet in general should immediately understand 1) what this argument does, 2) why this is an option in the constructor in the first place.

The API, the options made available to the user, should be about solving the user's problems, not about describing the underlying implementation, or routing around implementation issues.

In addition, in a good API, you don't need to use enums when strings work just as well, e.g. 'same', 'explicit'.

I believe this argument should not exist, as it does not provide value to a potential user.
